text: The surgeon retracts the left lobe of the liver and uses a Babcock clamp below the cardia to pull the esophagus downwards and laterally. The gastrohepatic ligament is divided, followed by the anterior phrenoesophageal ligament, exposing the anterior surface of the esophagus. The surgeon then develops the plane between the right crus and the esophagus, entering the retroesophageal plane and further developing it through blunt dissection.

The anterior surface of the esophagus is exposed by dividing the anterior frenoesophageal ligament. Approximately seven centimeters of the esophagus above the gastroesophageal (GE) junction is exposed using blunt dissection trans-hiatally, with downward traction applied using a Babcock clamp. The GE junction pad of fat is carefully dissected away from the bowel wall, revealing the cardia for two to three centimeters below the GE junction. As the fat pad is mobilized, the bare area of the cardia is visible, showing muscle fibers of the stomach without serosal covering, which will be split in the lower part of the myotomy.

The surgeon completes the exposure of the medial surface of the esophagus, extending from three centimeters below the gastroesophageal junction to seven centimeters above it. The esophageal myotomy begins with the splitting of esophageal fibers using two blunt instruments to apply lateral traction, revealing the smooth, glistening esophageal mucosal surface. The surgeon then creates a plane between the esophageal muscle fibers and mucosa through gentle blunt dissection, followed by dividing the esophageal muscles using a harmonic scalpel for a quick, complete, and bloodless myotomy.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the esophageal fibers, ensuring the inactive blade faces the mucosa. The myotomy progresses upwards along the esophagus, splitting approximately seven to eight centimeters of esophageal muscle above the GE junction. After completing the esophageal myotomy, the procedure shifts to the gastric part, extending three centimeters below the GE junction.

The surgeon splits the gastric circular, longitudinal, and oblique muscle fibers using blunt dissection techniques. In cases with previous procedures like balloon dilation, the oblique fibers may adhere densely to the mucosa, requiring careful blunt stripping and stretching to avoid mucosal injury. After completing the esophageal myotomy, the puncture test is performed by filling saline in the upper quadrant and instilling air into the esophagus through a Ryle's tube to check for any mucosal breaches, which are closed with a PDA stitch if detected.

Following the completion of the myotomy and puncture test, the surgeon performs an anterior 180-degree Dor fundoplication. The partial fundoplication involves wrapping the gastric fundus around the anterior aspect of the esophagus to prevent reflux, while ensuring the esophageal walls remain apart.

The surgeon fixes the gastric fundal patch by suturing it to both the medial cartilage of the esophagus and the medial crus of the diaphragm. A non-absorbable Ethibond suture is used for the fundoplication, with a slip knot technique demonstrated for tightening. The completed Heller's cardiomyotomy is shown with the anterior door fundoplication in place.
